topic: Someone discovers their recipe has become famous under another name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the glowing screen, the notification a tiny, brightly colored intrusion into the quiet hum of her kitchen. "The Lavender Honey Scone" was topping the trending charts. She picked up her lukewarm tea, swirling the dregs as if the heat might somehow translate into excitement. She’d spent three hours perfecting that recipe—a simple combination of buttermilk, browned butter, and a whisper of dried lavender. Now, some stranger was raving about it, using a slightly altered description. Eleanor just sighed, the sound barely audible over the distant traffic. She didn't feel a spark of triumph, nor did she feel a pang of jealousy. It was just another thing happening in the world, a fleeting ripple she wasn't part of. She set the mug down with a soft clink, already mentally moving on to the crossword puzzle waiting on the counter.

[story 2]

Mark leaned against the counter, arms crossed, watching the phone screen flicker with reviews. "The Midnight Bloom Bake," the title read. He watched the numbers climb, the accolades piling up. He picked up a spoon, turning it over in his fingers, the metal cool against his skin. He’d made that recipe years ago, a quiet indulgence for rainy afternoons. Seeing it packaged, marketed, and celebrated by someone else felt… unremarkable. He took a slow sip of his coffee, letting the bitterness settle. There was no surge of pride, just a steady, flat acknowledgment of the success. He shrugged, the movement barely disturbing his posture. It was just food, really. A collection of ingredients arranged in a specific sequence.

[story 3]

Sarah traced the faded handwriting on the old recipe card tucked into the cookbook. The name on the cover—"The Golden Crumb"—was now plastered across billboards and social media feeds. She looked up, her gaze drifting across the cluttered living room. She didn't feel the usual rush of elation one might expect when a creation gains traction. Instead, a strange detachment settled over her. She picked up a stray napkin, smoothed it flat, and then placed it back down without much thought. "Well," she murmured to the empty room, her voice even, "that’s that, then." She turned her attention to a book lying open on the side table, the words seeming infinitely more engaging than the sudden, loud fanfare about her old creation.
